cards.front: What are the stability conditions for P(s)=s^2+a1 s+a0?
cards.back: The system is stable if a1 > 0 and a0 > 0.

cards.front: What is the first-column sequence for the second-order Routh array?
cards.back: For a monic quadratic it is 1, a1, a0.

cards.front: What are the core third-order Routh conditions from the note?
cards.back: For P(s)=s^3+a2 s^2+a1 s+a0, stability requires a2 > 0, a0 > 0, and a1 a2 > a0.

cards.front: Why is positivity alone not enough for a third-order polynomial?
cards.back: Because the Routh first column also depends on the combined quantity a1a2-a0, not only on the individual signs.

cards.front: What is the third-order middle first-column entry before simplification?
What equivalent form of the third-order inequality also appears in the note?
cards.back: a1 - a0/a2 > 0, assuming a2 is positive.

cards.front: What do the second- and third-order rules really come from?
cards.back: They are compact versions of the general Routh array test, not isolated formulas to memorize blindly.

cards.front: If a2 > 0 and a0 > 0 but a1 a2 < a0, what is the conclusion?
cards.back: The system is unstable because the first column cannot remain entirely positive.

cards.front: Why are low-order Routh tests useful in exams?
cards.back: They let you classify stability quickly without rebuilding the full array when the denominator is second or third order.

cards.front: What habit should you form when you see a cubic denominator?
cards.back: Check coefficient positivity first, then immediately test whether a1 a2 > a0 before declaring stability.
